one_line_takeaway: Reproducing and substantially extending a prior LLM-ranker prompt-injection study, this paper shows the vulnerability is real, scales (non-monotonically) with model capability, is position- and domain-sensitive, and can be structurally mitigated -- Flan-T5's encoder-decoder architecture is an order of magnitude more robust than decoder-only or MoE rankers of comparable scale, while MoE offers no inherent protection.
key_finding: Reproduction largely confirms Qian et al.'s core claim (mean absolute ASR deviation <6% in pairwise/setwise; listwise required a corrected DOH prompt after author consultation). Four new/refined boundary conditions are reported: (1) Scaling is confirmed but non-monotonic and family-dependent -- e.g., Qwen3-0.6B reaches near-perfect 99.9% ASR under Pairwise-DOH (exceeding all larger Qwen3 variants) and 75.29% under Pairwise-DCH, a 71.95-point jump over Qwen3-1.7B's 3.34%; at the system level, mean nDCG@10 drops from 0.6584 (baseline) to 0.2947 across attack settings, a 55.2% relative decline, and larger/more capable models show steeper degradation. (2) Position sensitivity is real and paradigm-dependent, contradicting Qian et al.'s claim of position-agnosticism: for 14 of 15 evaluated backbones, back-placed injections cause significantly larger nDCG@10 degradation than front-placed ones (mean gap 0.1106, paired t-test p<0.05), even though raw ASR position effects are more mixed (e.g., Qwen3-32B Pairwise-DCH ASR drops from 95.36% back to 57.91% front). (3) Architecture matters: encoder-decoder Flan-T5-Large/XL show mean ASR of only 3.21%/3.28% (an order of magnitude below scale-matched decoder-only models, e.g., 15.6x lower than Qwen3-0.6B) and even negative nDCG@10 'degradation' (slight improvement) under attack, while the MoE model Qwen3-30B-A3B (84.83% mean ASR) is statistically indistinguishable from its dense scale-matched counterpart Qwen3-32B (83.62%), i.e., MoE routing confers no protection. Flan-T5 also fails to produce valid listwise outputs regardless of attack, a format-compatibility failure rather than a robustness gain. (4) Vulnerability generalizes across domains (TREC-DL vs. BEIR): Pearson r=0.969 (p=0.0003) between general and domain-specific ASR, R^2=0.938, with DBpedia (shortest docs, 49.7 words avg) most susceptible and SciFact (binary relevance) most resistant among BEIR sets -- but despite DBpedia's higher ASR, TREC-DL-2019 shows worse nDCG@10 degradation, an ASR/nDCG dissociation attributed to vulnerability scaling with model capacity.
core_contribution: A reproducibility-and-extension study of prompt-injection ('decision hijacking') attacks against LLM-based text rankers, originally reported by Qian et al. (2025, 'The Ranking Blind Spot'). The paper's own contribution is NOT the attack itself (that is prior work being reproduced) but: (1) a faithful reproduction of Qian et al.'s Preference Vulnerability results (ASR) across pairwise/setwise/listwise ranking paradigms on TREC-DL-2019/2020, including a corrected DOH listwise prompt found via consultation with the original authors; and (2) four new/refined empirical findings obtained by systematically extending the evaluation along axes the original study under-explored: (a) vulnerability scaling across model families (Qwen3, Gemma-3) and sizes (0.6B-70B) plus a new architecture axis (encoder-decoder Flan-T5, MoE Qwen3-30B-A3B); (b) injection-position sensitivity (front vs. back); (c) cross-domain robustness on four BEIR datasets (TREC-COVID, Touche-2020, SciFact, DBpedia) versus TREC-DL; and (d) a new second evaluation task, 'Ranking Vulnerability Assessment', that measures operational nDCG@10 degradation in a full BM25+LLM-reranking pipeline, rather than relying only on the original study's intrinsic preference-flip metric (ASR).
threat_model: Adversary controls (or can inject text into) candidate documents that a retrieval system will pass to an LLM-based re-ranker; the adversary's goal is to have their (non-relevant) document ranked as most relevant/top of the list via a jailbreak-style prompt injection embedded in the document text, using two attack variants inherited from Qian et al.: Decision Objective Hijacking (DOH, instructs the model to output the injected document as most relevant) and Decision Criteria Hijacking (DCH, redefines the model's relevance criteria to favor a marker in the injected passage). The paper explicitly flags this threat model as somewhat unrealistic in its Conclusion: it assumes the attacker knows which documents were retrieved as non-relevant by the first-stage retriever and can inject the payload into all of them simultaneously -- conditions 'often unlikely to occur in practice.'
limitations: The authors themselves state the attack's threat model requires knowledge of which candidates the first-stage retriever marked non-relevant and the ability to inject into all of them -- conditions they call unlikely in practice -- and call for future work on more realistic attacker capabilities (e.g., SEO-style single-document injection). Task 2's setwise-only design (chosen to model a 'worst case' and limit compute) means nDCG@10 degradation results are not validated across pairwise/listwise paradigms. The listwise DOH results in the reproducibility table were affected by an error in the original attack prompt (fixed via author consultation), and listwise DOH is excluded from later analyses because the fix broke comparability with pairwise/setwise DOH. Gemma-3-1B is excluded from Task 2 analysis because it produces largely invalid output and underperforms BM25 even without attack. Minor unexplained reproduction discrepancies remain for Qwen3-1.7B/8B, attributed to unspecified environmental differences (vLLM version 0.11.0 vs. 0.8.5, bf16 vs. original precision, independently implemented serving/hosting layer).

datasets_used: MS MARCO passages (context for average document length comparison)
quantitative_results: Reproducibility: mean absolute ASR deviation from Qian et al. <6% for pairwise and setwise settings
Qwen3-0.6B: 99.9% ASR under Pairwise-DOH (highest among all Qwen3 sizes tested)
Qwen3-0.6B: 75.29% ASR under Pairwise-DCH vs. Qwen3-1.7B's 3.34% (71.95-point gap)
Mean system-level nDCG@10 drops from 0.6584 (baseline across models) to 0.2947 under attack (55.2% relative decline)
Position sensitivity in Task 2: back-placed injection significantly worse than front for 14/15 backbones, mean nDCG@10 gap 0.1106 (paired t-test p<0.05)
Flan-T5-Large mean ASR 3.21%, Flan-T5-XL mean ASR 3.28% (pairwise+setwise, DOH+DCH, back+front)
Qwen3-0.6B up to 15.6x higher vulnerability than Flan-T5-Large at ~0.8B scale
Qwen3-30B-A3B (MoE) mean ASR 84.83% vs. Qwen3-32B (dense) 83.62% -- statistically indistinguishable
Cross-domain ASR correlation (TREC-DL vs. BEIR): Pearson r=0.969 (p=0.0003), R^2=0.938
DBpedia average document length 49.7 words (most susceptible); Touche-2020 292.4 words; TREC-COVID 160.8 words; SciFact 213.6 words; MS MARCO 56.0 words
Task 2 reproduction: LLaMA-3-8B baseline nDCG@10 0.6602 (theirs 0.6930), DOH-back attacked drop to 0.1569 (theirs 0.1050)
Mean DOH vs. DCH nDCG@10 degradation difference: 0.0090 (marginal but often statistically significant, paired t-test p<0.05)
LLaMA-3.3-70B and GPT-4.1-mini: near-100% ASR across pairwise/setwise/listwise in reproduced results (e.g., Llama-3.3-70B Pairwise DOH 100.00%, GPT-4.1-mini Setwise DCH 99.98%)
baselines_compared: Qian et al. (2025) 'The Ranking Blind Spot' original ASR results (reproduced side-by-side in Table 3)
Safe (unattacked) LLM reranking vs. Attacked (DOH/DCH) reranking, per model
Scale-matched architecture comparisons: Flan-T5-Large vs. Qwen3-0.6B vs. Gemma-3-1B (~0.8B tier); Flan-T5-XL vs. Qwen3-1.7B vs. Gemma-3-4B (~3B tier); Qwen3-32B vs. Qwen3-30B-A3B (MoE) (~32B tier)
